dioceseInfo: Climate- The soil and the climate of Georgia invite settlers to come here and work to their own advantage the agricultural resources of the State, which are inexhaustible. Catholics are not advised to settle in the midst of sectarian districts, far from any Catholic church, where they and their children would be exposed to the danger of shipwreck in the faith. But if they understand gardening, and have a small capital, they will find, in the vicinity of cities, rich lots, which will repay well their industry.

There are Catholic schools attached to nearly all the churches and chapels throughout the diocese.

There are Catholic schools attached to nearly all the churches and chapels throughout the diocese.
There are schools attached to all the German and most of the other churches in the diocese.

In Louisville there are conferences of St. Vincent of Paul, connected with the cathedral, St. John's Church, St, Patrick's, St. Michael's, and St. Louis Bertrand's, numbering in all about 500 members, laboring with great zeal, and doing much good. The German congregations have also benevolent and mutual relief societies. The cathedral congregation has a charitable association of ladies under the title Mary and Martha Society, designed to procure clothing for the children of the poor.
Conferences of St. Vincent de Paul's Society are established in nearly all the city parishes

There are 12 to 14 stations in other parts of Georgia, which are visited once or twice in the year.  Six or eight other stations in South Carolina are occasionally visited by the Bishop or one of his clergymen.  Several other stations in North Carolina are occasionally visited.

Sunday schools in most of the churches. Two of the priests of the diocese are temporarily absent, Rev. Henry Malone and Rev. Henry Hoffman.
N.B. - Just arrived, six priests of the Order of the Resurrection, for the station of Paña-Maria, Banders, and Martinez

editorialNote: Note from the editors: Details about individual schools in the Dubuque diocese are unknown because the almanac lists schools in clusters by city, especially pre-1869.  We have assigned IDs that connect school clusters in the same city over multiple years.  However, it is very possible that individual schools opened and closed each year, and those specific institutions are not individually recorded in the almanac.  One will need to consult outside sources rather than the almanacs themselves if one is concerned about whether a particular school endured from year to year rather than the general knowledge of Catholic schools in general existing in a particular city year to year.
